input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
Here's how to make your page theme-aware:

## XAML — `AppThemeBinding`

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}" />

        <Label Text="This page adapts to your system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

`AppThemeBinding` watches `Application.Current.RequestedTheme` and swaps values automatically when the OS theme changes — no event handling needed.

---

## C# alternative — `SetAppTheme`

The equivalent in code-behind or C# markup:

```csharp
// Extension method on any BindableObject
var page = new ContentPage();
page.SetAppTheme(
    ContentPage.BackgroundColorProperty,
    Colors.White,   // Light
    Color.FromArgb("#1E1E1E")); // Dark

var label = new Label { Text = "Welcome", FontSize = 28 };
label.SetAppTheme(
    Label.TextColorProperty,
    Colors.Black,
    Color.FromArgb("#E0E0E0"));

var button = new Button { Text = "Get Started" };
button.SetAppTheme(Button.BackgroundColorProperty,
    Color.FromArgb("#6200EE"),
    Color.FromArgb("#BB86FC"));
button.SetAppTheme(Button.TextColorProperty,
    Colors.White,
    Colors.Black);
```

`SetAppTheme(BindableProperty, lightValue, darkValue)` does exactly what `AppThemeBinding` does in XAML.

---

## Scaling up with resource dictionaries

For real apps, avoid scattering hex codes everywhere. Define themed resources in `App.xaml`:

```xml
<Application.Resources>
    <Color x:Key="PageBg">{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}</Color>
    <Color x:Key="PrimaryText">{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}</Color>
</Application.Resources>
```

Then reference them:

```xml
<ContentPage BackgroundColor="{StaticResource PageBg}">
    <Label TextColor="{StaticResource PrimaryText}" />
</ContentPage>
```

---

**Key points:**
- Reacts live to OS theme changes — no restart needed.
- Works on all platforms (iOS, Android, Windows, Mac Catalyst).
- If you ever need to force a theme, set `Application.Current.UserAppTheme = AppTheme.Dark;`.
